coredata.dc:title: A self-quenching-resistant carbon dots powder with tunable solid-state fluorescence and their applications in light-emitting diodes and fingerprints detection
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Hai-Jiao
Yu, Ting-Ting
Chen, Hong-Li
Nan, Wen-Bin
Xie, Li-Qin
Zhang, Qi-Qing
coredata.dc:description: In this report, a novel type of carbon dots (CDs) were prepared via a simple MW-assisted method by using citric acid and piperazine as precursors. The CDs can be obtained in large-scale and emit bright solid-state fluorescence (SSF) with different colors. Then the synthetic CDs were fully characterized with PL, UV, XPS, TEM, and FTIR and the relationship between the mass ratio of precursors and physicochemical property of the CDs was also investigated detailedly. The results showed that these CDs prepared at different mass ratios have different element content and functional group content. More importantly, the mass ratio of the precursors not only has a great influence on their particle sizes, but also can affect their PL properties. With the decreased of the mass ratio, their particle size decreased gradually and their emission wavelength gradually red-shift. This interesting finding may be helpful for fabricating CDs with controllable particle size and tunable light-emitting colors. Based on their excellent SSF property, these CDs were successfully used to fabricate light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and detect latent fingerprints (LFPs). In conclusion, the research may offer a valuable reference for obtaining CDs with SSF and developing LEDs phosphors and LFPs detection reagent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Solid-state fluorescence
Light-emitting diodes
Latent fingerprints

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of LEDs and visualization of latent fingerprints

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Start-up Fund of Natural Science Foundation
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank financial support from the PhD Start-up Fund of Natural Science Foundation (No. 505244 ) and National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 31600791 , 51503175 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration for the synthesis CDs and photographs of the CDs under daylight and UV light. (b)–(d) TEM images of A1, A2 and A3, respectively.
(a)–(c) XPS spectra of A1, A2 and A3, respectively. (d) FT-IR spectra of CDs.
(a)–(c) PL spectra of the CDs in solid state. (d)–(f) Excitation-dependent PL spectra of the CDs in solid state.

(a) Photograph of the working LEDs (from left to right: A1, A2 and A3-based LED with 365 nm UV chip and A2-based LED with 395 nm UV chip). (b) The corresponding PL emission spectrum of the above LED-devices. (c) The corresponding CIE coordinates of the CDs.

(a) Photographs of CDs stained fingerprints on different substrate (Glass, Plastics, Tinfoil and Weighing paper) under UV light. (b) The corresponding magnified pictures of above CDs stained fingerprints.

head.ce:title: A self-quenching-resistant carbon dots powder with tunable solid-state fluorescence and their applications in light-emitting diodes and fingerprints detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science and Technology, Xinxiang Medical University, Xinxiang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Preventive Medicine, Changzhi Medical College, Changzhi, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biomedical Engineering, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences, Tianjin, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Life Science and Technology, Xinxiang Medical University, Xinxiang, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this report, a novel type of carbon dots (CDs) were prepared via a simple MW-assisted method by using citric acid and piperazine as precursors. The CDs can be obtained in large-scale and emit bright solid-state fluorescence (SSF) with different colors. Then the synthetic CDs were fully characterized with PL, UV, XPS, TEM, and FTIR and the relationship between the mass ratio of precursors and physicochemical property of the CDs was also investigated detailedly. The results showed that these CDs prepared at different mass ratios have different element content and functional group content. More importantly, the mass ratio of the precursors not only has a great influence on their particle sizes, but also can affect their PL properties. With the decreased of the mass ratio, their particle size decreased gradually and their emission wavelength gradually red-shift. This interesting finding may be helpful for fabricating CDs with controllable particle size and tunable light-emitting colors. Based on their excellent SSF property, these CDs were successfully used to fabricate light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and detect latent fingerprints (LFPs). In conclusion, the research may offer a valuable reference for obtaining CDs with SSF and developing LEDs phosphors and LFPs detection reagent.

Carbon dots powder with tunable solid-state fluorescence was prepared by large-scale via a simple MW-assisted method and also successfully applied to fabricate LED and LFPs detection.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: As a newly emerging star in fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn tremendous attentions in nanotechnology field. Since the first report in 2004, various types of CDs have been synthesized for chemical/biological sensing, imaging, nanomedicine, photocatalysis and electrocatalysis [1]. CDs have many captivating properties, such as excellent photo-stability, non-blinking emission, tunable excitation and emission wavelength, upconversion photoluminescence, favorable biocompatibility and good water solubility. These features make CDs to be alternatives to conventional fluorescent organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots. However, like many organic dyes, the fluorescence of CDs always undergo aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) in the solid state due to excessive resonance energy transfer (RET) or direct π-π interactions [2]. Besides, solid CDs are also difficult to obtain because of hydroscopicity. Therefore, to data, most CDs-related reports are focused on the fluorescence and application in their liquid state and only few reports have just mentioned solid-state fluorescence (SSF) of CDs powder [2–4]. These drawbacks may largely limit the use of CDs in numerous applications, such as optoelectronic devices and sensors preparation, which generally require photoluminescent materials emitting in the solid state.

In order to overcome the ACQ effect of CDs, some methods have been proposed. Such as Liu et al. developed a one-pot sol-gel method to achieve solid-state light emission of CDs [5]. They thought that silica can serve as a dispersion medium and the link between two photoluminescent species, because of silica can effectively prevent the CDs from aggregating and evenly distribute the CDs over the phosphor to prevent phase separation. Recently, Qu et al. presented that the CDs-triggered silica gelation can effectively prevent the collision and aggregation among CDs and resulting in strongly fluorescence emission in CDs@silica composite gels with highly concentrated emitting centers [6]. While, Shen et al. used tween 80 to prepare CDs with white fluorescence in solid-state and they proposed the presence of several long alkyl chains on the surface of the as-fabricated CDs can effectively suppress the ACQ effect and finally resulting in emitting bright white luminescence under an UV illumination of 365 nm in the solid states [7]. Other methods include using agarose, sylgard 184 silicone elastomer base (PDMS base), PMMA, polyvinyl butyral, sodium borosilicate and epoxy resin as solid scaffold to suppress the ACQ effect, are also often reported [8–12]. However, the method to directly constructed powdery solid light-emitting CDs without further treatment to conquer ACQ effect was seldom reported.

Herein, we developed a facile method that using cyclic amine piperazine as passivant and citric acid as carbon source to directly fabricate CDs with SSF property. The method is very simple and only need 2 min microwave irradiation in domestic microwave oven. The powdery CDs can be directly obtained in a large scale by slightly and gently grinding the reaction product after microwave irradiation. Based on this method, three new type of CDs with different solid-light emitting colors were prepared by altering the mass ratio of precursors. Meanwhile, the relationship between the mass ratio of precursors and SSF property was also studied detailedly. Furthermore, based on their SSF property, these CDs were successfully applied for fabricating light-emitting diodes (LED) and latent fingerprints detection.

Citric acid and piperazine were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received. All reagents were used as received without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experiment. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed on a FT-IR Nicolet 380 spectrometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were collected with a ESCALB MK-II photoelectron spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on JEOL, JEM-2100F at operating voltage of 200 kV. Fluorescence emission spectra were obtained using a FluoroMax-4 Spectrofluorophotometer (HORIBA JobinYvon) at 298 K. The LED were recorded using a Cary eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer with Xe lamp as an excitation source.

Briefly, citric acid (2 g) and piperazine with three mass ratio (w/w = 1: 0.5, 1: 1, 1: 2) were dissolved in 5 mL deionized water in the 100 mL beaker. Then the beaker was placed at the center of the rotation plate of a domestic microwave oven (700 W) and heated for 2 min. After cooling down, The three CDs powder were obtained by slightly and gently grinding the solid CDs without further purification steps and named as A1, A2 and A3, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of LEDs and visualization of latent fingerprints
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, CDs (70 mg) were uniformly mixed with the silicone (555 mg) in an ep tubes and the bubbles were removed by ultrasonic. Then the CDs/silicone mixtures were dispensed on the LED chip and thermally cured at 60 °C for 40 min and at 135 °C for 90 min. For latent fingerprints detection, a squirrel-hair brush was used to apply the CDs powders to fingerprints deposited on the substrated. The fluorescence microscopy images were obtained under 365 nm UV light.

As it is exhibited in Fig. 1 a, three solid CDs were directly prepared through facile one-step MW-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and piperazine. Then the CDs powder were obtained by slightly and gently grinding the solid CDs after microwave irradiation without further complex purification steps, which means large-scale synthesis could be realized with low cost. Under UV light (365 nm), these three CDs can emit strong blue, light blue, yellow-green colors, respectively. However, it is very strange that their fluorescence intensity was very weak in aqueous solution (Fig. S1). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were carried out to study the morphology of these CDs (Fig. 1b–d). The results revealed that the morphology of three CDs are regular spherical particles dispersing in water. The average particle size were measured to be 7.8 ± 1.2 nm, 3.8 ± 0.7 nm and 3.2 ± 0.7 nm for A1, A2 and A3, respectively (Fig. S2). As some reports ascribed the red shift of CD emission to the quantum confinement effect, the different average particle size of these CDs may cause different fluorescence emission peaks [13]. In high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images, both A1 and A2 showed an obvious lattice fringe. However, the lattice fringe of A3 was shadowy. The results may indicate A1 and A2 are highly crystalline, while the crystallinity of A3 seems to be poor. The lattice fringe spacings of these CDs were measured to about 0.30 nm, 0.25 nm and 0.22 nm, respectively, which were in close matched with that of the (002), (1120) and (100) planes within the graphite, respectively, indicating that the graphite-like structure of as-synthesized crystalline core of CDs [14–17]. X-ray Diffraction (XRD) (Fig. S3) displays a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = ∼18° and a wide feeble peak at 2θ = ∼32°, confirming that the structures of CDs are composed of small crystalline cores with disordered surface [18,19]. Interestingly, with the decrease of mass ratio, both the average particle size and lattice fringe spacings of these CDs decreased gradually. To the best our knowledge, this unusual phenomenon has not been reported in the previous literature. To understand this phenomenon, we assume there exist three steps in the whole pyrolysis process according to previous reports, that is, (1) starting material dehydration and the core of CDs formation, (2) surface passivation of core of CDs (amide bond formation) and (3) growth of core of CDs. The three steps have no well-defined boundary and might occur synchronously [18]. Therefore, when a large amount of amine groups from piperazine were passivated to the surface of the core of CDs, numerous amide bonds will be formed, which will prevent the continuous growth of the CDs and resulting in smaller particle size.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra was used to investigate the chemical composition and chemical bonding of the CDs (Fig. 2 a–c). The XPS full survey spectra showed three prominent features of C1s at 285 eV, N1s at 400 eV and O1s at 532 eV. In the high-resolution spectra, the C1s spectrum can be resolved into four peaks at 284.7 eV, 286.0 eV and 287.8 eV, which were attributed to C–C/C=C, C–O/C-N and C=O groups respectively [19,20]. N1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into two peaks at ∼399.7 eV and ∼401.2 eV, which should be contributed to the presence of pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N, respectively [21,22]. The O1s spectrum of these three CDs also exhibited two oxygen states of C=O at 530.6 eV and C-OH/C-O-C at 531.7 eV [23,24]. Furthermore, the elemental content of the C, N and O were also calculated from the XPS data (Table. S1). Obviously, with the decrease of mass ratio, the content of the N element increased gradually. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra was carried out to further characterize the surface groups of these CDs. As shown in Fig. 2d, these prepared CDs showed similar transmittance peaks. The peaks at 3200–3450 cm−1 are corresponding to the O-H and N-H stretching vibrations [25–27]. The peaks at 1722 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 were attributed to stretching vibrations of carboxyl's C=O and vibrations of amide's C=O, respectively [28,29]. Meanwhile, with the decrease of mass ratio, the peak intensity of amide's C=O increased gradually compared to the peak intensity of carboxyl's C=O, suggesting that more N atoms were doped in CDs to form amide bond at lower mass ratio.

The UV–Vis absorption of these CDs was researched at room temperature and as shown in Fig. S4, all of them had an absorption peak at 225 nm corresponding to the π → π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain. With the decrease of mass ratio, some new absorption peaks were observed, such as the absorption peak at 296 nm for A2 and A3, which attributed to the n−π* transitions of C=O bonds, and absorption peaks at 350 nm and 488 nm for A3, corresponding to the n–p* transitions arising from the edge states formed at the boundary of the sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbons and the electron transition of the surface states. These results may indicate the mass ratio of citric acid and piperazine can have a great influence on the optical properties of these CDs.

Then the photoluminescence emission (PL) spectra of these CDs were evaluated at 365 nm in solid state (Fig. 3 a–c). Results showed that A1 had only one emission peak with the maximum emission wavelength (λmaxem) at 436 nm. While, in addition to the emission peak at 436 nm, A2 and A3 also had another fluorescence peak with the λmaxem at 503 nm and 530 nm, respectively. The fluorescent emissions of CDs may depend on the λex and Fig. 3d–f showed the PL spectra recorded for three CDs with excitation wavelength ranged from 365 to 435 nm. Obviously, with the increase of excitation wavelength, the fluorescence intensity of A1 decreased remarkably with a red-shift of emission wavelength. Such results indicated that the CDs in solid sate also can have excitation-dependent feature, which is in agreement with previous investigations in aqueous solution [1,30–32]. On the other hand, for A2 and A3, their fluorescence intensity at high-energy UV region in the PL spectra firstly decreased and then increased, while the fluorescence intensity at low-energy UV region increased continuously. Furthermore, the PL spectra of these CDs in aqueous solution were also measured (Fig. S5). Similar to their PL spectra in solid state, A2 and A3 still displayed two emission peak. Interestingly, with the increase of the excitation wavelength from 365 to 435 nm, the fluorescence intensity in the high-energy UV region gradually decreased and the corresponding fluorescence peaks even disappeared. While their fluorescence intensity in low-energy UV region increased gradually, which was similar to their fluorescence emission under solid state.

Although the PL mechanisms for CDs remain poorly understand and needs scientists to conduct deeper explorations. So far, some PL mechanisms of CDs still have been proposed and can be divided into two categories: one is based on the band gap transitions in conjugated π-domains, which originates from the quantum confinement effect and the other is related to surface defects on the CDs [13,20]. Based the fluorescence behavior of these CDs in solid state and aqueous solution, we believed that quantum-confinement effects may not be the key parameter that determines their PL properties, either. Otherwise, the PL emission peaks of these CDs would take place red-shift with increased sizes [13,33–35]. Therefore, in our case, we speculated that the surface defects would be the dominant factor for the origins of their PL. In addition, the double emission property of A2 and A3 may strongly hint that A1 has only one type of surface defects, while A2 and A3 may have two types of surface defects. One type surface defects in A2 and A3 is corresponding to the fluorescence emission in high-energy UV region and another one is corresponding to the fluorescence emission in low-energy UV region.

Light-emitting diodes (LEDs) have become the most popular illumination element in recent years. As their strong fluorescence in solid state, these prepared CDs are used as light-emitting phosphors for fabricating LED by coating the CDs onto commercially UV LEDs. As shown in Fig. 4 , all the three CDs can transfer the UV light to the visible region resulting in strong visible light emission. The CIE coordinates were also calculated from the emission spectra of the CDs-based LEDs (Fig. 4b and c). The results showed that A1 and A2-based LED had the CIE coordinates of (0.34, 0.45) and (0.33, 0.44), respectively, indicating the green-yellow light emission was achieved with the 365 nm UV chip. Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission of A3-based LED was concentrated in the yellow light region with a CIE coordinates of (0.44, 0.50), meaning warm yellow light emitting. Furthermore, the A2-based LED with 395 nm UV chip had a CIE coordinates of (0.25, 0.28), suggesting the cold white light emitting. Other related parameters of LED were also measured (Table S2) and all the above results demonstrated that these CDs may serve as a promising candidate for LEDs.

Fluorescence staining is a general method for the detection of latent fingerprints (LFPs) with high sensitivity [36]. Therefore, the prepared A3 with strong green-yellow SSF were also used to LFPs detection by using the conventional powder technique [37]. Fig. 5 a and b showed the PL images of sebaceous fingerprints developed by A3. Apparently, the fingerprint patterns are clearly observed with high-quality under UV light (365 nm), in which the papillary ridges are lighted up. Furthermore, the second-level details, such as the ridge termination and bifurcation, can be also recognized and distinguished (Fig. 5b). These details form the basis of fingerprint identification in principle, and they are critical in practical identification [38]. Such results confirm the CDs are versatile and have the great potential in practical applications.

Overall, we successfully established a simple strategy for synthesizing CDs by large-scale. The preparation method did not need complex post-processing steps and was very simple and low-cost. The prepared CDs can effectively suppress the ACQ effect and emit bright SSF under UV illumination. The results showed the mass ratio of citric acid and piperazine not only can affect the particle size of the CDs, but also can affect their PL property, which will be beneficial to obtained CDs with different size and different visible color lights. Finally, based on their strong SSF property, these CDs were successfully applied for fabricating LEDs and the fluorescence imaging of latent fingerprints. The study not only provides a promising new method to obtain SSF-emitting CDs, but also shows the developed CDs have the great potential in diverse practical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank financial support from the PhD Start-up Fund of Natural Science Foundation (No.505244) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (31600791, 51503175).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2018.06.039.
